rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active but controllable haemorrhage (a slow venous ooze, haemodynamically stable) from a suspected skin malignancy on the nasal ala, in a frail elderly nursing-home resident on antiplatelet therapy, where basic nursing measures had not achieved hemostasis. Definitive hemostasis (chemical cautery / professional dressing) and a biopsy/excision referral pathway require URGENT same-day in-person care — telehealth cannot cauterise, dress or biopsy. It is not an immediate threat to life (no shock, no airway/circulatory compromise, slow ooze) so it is NOT T4/T5; but it is too acute and too suspicious for routine-only management, so T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care) is correct. Escalate to ED only if bleeding is not controlled by sustained pressure.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bleeding not controlled after a full 20 minutes of firm continuous direct pressure, or the dressing saturating and dripping continuously
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If firm pressure for 20 minutes fails to control it, arrange transfer to the emergency department for surgical hemostasis
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled bleeding despite adequate first aid, prolonged by antiplatelet therapy, requires definitive surgical/procedural hemostasis that only an ED or in-person proceduralist can provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding hasn't stopped after 20 minutes of firm, unbroken pressure — or the dressing soaks through and keeps dripping — the nurses should arrange transfer to the emergency department for the bleeding to be sealed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of significant blood loss — dizziness or lightheadedness, pallor, a fast or pounding heartbeat, feeling faint or clammy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek emergency assessment the same hour if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate the ooze has caused clinically meaningful blood loss in a frail patient and needs urgent assessment and hemostasis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the patient becomes dizzy, pale, faint or her heart starts racing, treat it as significant blood loss — get her to the emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, loss of consciousness, or torrential/spurting bleeding that cannot be controlled at all
escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse or truly uncontrollable haemorrhage is a life-threatening emergency requiring an ambulance; this is the ceiling escalation, not the baseline response to a slow controllable ooze.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she collapses, becomes unresponsive, or the bleeding is heavy and simply will not stop with pressure, call 000 for an ambulance right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lesion itself — a non-healing, growing, ulcerating facial lump — regardless of the bleeding
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent in-person skin review and biopsy/excision referral within days to 1-2 weeks even after bleeding is controlled
escalation_edges.rationale: The suspicious lesion needs tissue diagnosis and excision planning independent of the acute bleed; controlling the bleeding does not close the case.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even once the bleeding settles, this lump needs an in-person skin doctor to look at it and take a small biopsy, and to plan removal — please make sure that review is booked.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult, residential aged-care resident, on daily low-dose aspirin
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep the threshold to confirm the bleeding is truly controlled low, and ensure on-site nursing support is arranged for pressure and dressing care; but also avoid an unnecessary ambulance/ED transfer, which is itself distressing and harmful for a frail resident when the bleed is controllable in place. Balance urgent lesion review against over-medicalising a stable patient.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A facility registered nurse is present and can perform pressure, dressing changes and monitoring
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Leverage the on-site RN to deliver firm direct pressure and ongoing wound care and to escalate if triggers are met — this supports safe management in place while urgent in-person clinician review is arranged.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Have the on-site nurse apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted pressure over gauze to the spot for a full 20 minutes without peeking, then keep a protective dressing dry and intact for about three days. Arrange an urgent same-day in-person review (facility GP visit, urgent care, or skin clinic) to seal the bleeding point properly and to organise a biopsy and removal of the lump. This is urgent but not an ambulance emergency while she is stable and the bleeding is a slow ooze.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day facility GP / urgent care / skin clinic review; call 000 only for collapse or uncontrollable bleeding
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If after hours and the bleeding is controlled with pressure and a dressing, arrange the earliest next in-person review; if the bleeding cannot be controlled by 20 minutes of firm pressure or she shows signs of significant blood loss, go to the emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document the lesion (two-year pearly growing nodule on the right nasal ala with central ulceration and contact bleeding), daily low-dose aspirin use, and request an urgent skin-cancer review for biopsy and excision planning given the high-risk facial site.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring and discharging with only self-care or routine advice for a suspicious, actively-bleeding facial malignancy in a frail anticoagulated patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A skin cancer is missed and left to grow and destroy local facial tissue, and an anticoagulated frail patient is left with an unresolved bleeding lesion and no referral.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as a routine 24-48 hour GP problem without arranging same-day in-person hemostasis when basic nursing measures had already failed to stop the bleed.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a haemodynamically stable patient with a slow controllable venous ooze to ED by ambulance / calling 000 as the baseline response — distressing emergency transfer of a frail resident and misuse of ambulance/ED resources for a bleed controllable in place.
